headline: Hidden Avocado Varieties Could Save the World's Favorite Fruit
meta_description: Scientists in Nicaragua found genetic treasure: native avocado varieties resistant to disease and climate stress, offering hope for a vulnerable global crop.
summary_short: Researchers discovered diverse avocado species in Central America that could protect the commercial industry from disease and climate threats.
body:
Researchers trudging through Nicaragua's rainforests have uncovered a genetic goldmine that could reshape global avocado farming. American scientists, led by archaeobotanist Kevin Wann from Texas A&M University, identified native avocado varieties—called landraces—that possess disease resistance and climate resilience missing in the supermarket staples we know. The team analyzed DNA from 47 native landraces, 32 of which came from Nicaragua, revealing genetic connections to South American avocados and exposing a "much wider breadth of avocado diversity that science had not yet documented." What makes this discovery urgent is the fragility of current supply chains: nearly all 11 million metric tons of avocados grown worldwide annually belong to a single variety, the Hass, produced through clonal grafting. This genetic uniformity means a single disease or climate shift could devastate the entire industry.

Local farmers in Nicaragua's lowlands have protected these native varieties for thousands of years through traditional agricultural practices, and their patience has paid off. Remarkably, the lowland avocado landraces had not experienced root rot, the most common disease plaguing commercial avocados. These regional varieties are also adapted to warmer, wetter climates—advantages increasingly valuable as weather patterns shift globally.

The research, published in Plants People Planet, reveals that humans themselves sparked this diversity 5,000 years ago when they transported avocados hundreds of miles from South America northward, allowing seeds to hybridize with native plants across Central America's humid lowlands. By tapping into this existing genetic treasure, scientists believe they can develop disease-resistant and climate-resilient commercial varieties, protecting both farmers and the multi-billion-dollar avocado industry from the fate that befell bananas—devastated by fungal pathogens in the past.
quick_facts: 47 native avocado landraces studied, 32 from Nicaragua alone
Nearly all global avocados are single Hass variety from clonal grafting
Local varieties show resistance to root rot, the most common avocado disease
Fruit traveled 5,000 years ago from South America to Central America via humans
